prompt:
Ticket for the edge-agent team. Our collector boxes can't run a real TSDB (128MB RAM budget) but the local rule evaluator needs the last ~30 minutes of samples queryable in-process. Build the storage core: module go-tseries, package tseries, stdlib only, this repo.

API the evaluator will call:
- NewStore(retention time.Duration, now func() time.Time) *Store — retention <= 0 means keep everything; nil now means time.Now. Every retention decision goes through that clock, nothing else — CI simulates hours in microseconds.
- Append(series string, t time.Time, v float64) error. Collectors flush late and out of order, that has to be fine. Empty series name is an error. Two appends with the identical series+timestamp: the later one wins in place (corrected readings), it is NOT a second point. Appending a sample that is already outside the retention window is an error and stores nothing.
- Query(series string, from, to time.Time) []Sample with Sample{T time.Time; V float64} — half-open [from, to), ascending by time. Unknown series or an empty/inverted window is just an empty result.
- Downsample(series string, from, to time.Time, bucket time.Duration) ([]Bucket, error) with Bucket{Start time.Time; Min, Max, Avg float64; Count int}. Buckets are aligned to `from` (from, from+bucket, ...), NOT to the epoch or wall clock. Buckets with no samples are omitted. bucket <= 0 is an error.
- Retention semantics: a sample is expired once t <= now-retention. Expired points must never appear in Query/Downsample/Len/Series output even if nothing has evicted them yet; Sweep() int actually frees them and reports how many it dropped.
- Len(series string) int (live points), Series() []string (sorted, only series that still have a live point).

The whole store gets hit from the collector goroutines and the evaluator at once, so it must be safe under -race. tseries_test.go and retention_test.go are the acceptance contract — don't touch them; go test -race -timeout 30s ./... has to pass. Split storage, downsampling, and retention into their own files; I don't want one giant store.go.
